content.assessment: Grade-splitting is a plausible political compromise that partially satisfies both centralizing efficiency logic and localist attachment to schools, but the coalition dynamics required to negotiate and durably maintain site-selection for consolidated upper grades are largely untested in the supplied evidence base for Hungary specifically. Existing findings speak more to the general instability of top-down reorganizations and the risk of contested siting decisions than to grade-splitting's specific feasibility.
opportunities: Could be positioned within a rural-revival political narrative similar to Hungary's Magyar Falu Program, which has demonstrated capacity to mobilize funding and political will for village-level investment (F-live-political_feasibility-04)

National mobilization patterns around education (teacher/parent coalitions) could be leveraged as advocates for retaining lower grades locally, since younger children are less exposed to closure (F-live-political_feasibility-08)
strengths: Preserves a visible local institution (the lower-grade building), which may reduce the intensity of village-level opposition compared to outright closure
Offers a partial answer to the specialist-teacher shortage that has driven consolidation pressure elsewhere, potentially building support among educators
threats: Long-run historical precedent of rapid, reversal-prone school-network changes (communist-era centralization, church-school nationalization) signals reversal risk for any new structural model, including grade-splitting (F-live-political_feasibility-09)

Documented local capacity for mobilization and reversal (Budapest 2025 case) suggests upper-grade consolidation could trigger similar resistance if communities perceive it as a partial closure (F-live-political_feasibility-07)

Depopulation severity in many villages (147 facing eventual full depopulation, 645 under 300 population) may render grade-splitting moot for a substantial subset of localities regardless of political design (F-live-political_feasibility-05)
weaknesses: No direct Hungarian political-feasibility evidence is supplied for grade-splitting as a model; existing findings address closure/centralization dynamics generally rather than this specific structural form
Site selection for the consolidated upper-grade school risks reviving the same inter-municipal competitive dynamics that have historically generated local political conflict
